.codex\I-safety-002b\codex_lock5.txt:3180:.\web\public\canonical_bundles\v1_canonical_success\evidence_pool.json:7:      "full_text": "Abstract This article reviews the efficacy and safety data of tirzepatide, a once-weekly, novel glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide and glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist approved in the United States, the European Union, and other regions for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. All doses of tirzepatide demonstrated superiority in reducing A1C and body weight from baseline versus placebo or active comparators. The safety profile of tirzepatide was consistent with that of the GLP-1 receptor agonist class, with mild to moderate and transient gastrointestinal side effects being the most common adverse events. With clinically and statistically significant reductions in A1C and body weight without increased risk of hypoglycemia in various populations, tirzepatide has demonstrated potential as a first-in-class treatment option for many people with type 2 diabetes. Type 2 diabetes is a metabolic condition caused by increased insulin resistance and progressive \u03b2-cell failure in the pancreas, resulting in decreased insulin secretion and hyperglycemia. Chronic hyperglycemia can ultimately lead to both micro- and macrovascular complications, which can be prevented with appropriate glycemic control. Obesity is a major factor in the pathophysiology of type 2 diabetes; therefore, treatment of type 2 diabetes should address hyperglycemia and comorbidities ([1](#B1),[2](#B2)). Glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) receptor agonists are commonly used as first injectable therapy\n\n[...]\n\nrther studies are needed to evaluate these potential mechanisms of action in humans. A phase 2 study assessed the efficacy and safety of four doses (1, 5, 10, and 15 mg) of tirzepatide versus placebo and an active comparator (once-weekly dulaglutide 1.5 mg) in patients with type 2 diabetes ([20](#B20)). The study demonstrated the superiority of tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg versus placebo and dulaglutide 1.5 mg in decreasing A1C at week 26. Likewise, tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg were superior to placebo in body weight reduction, and tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg were superior to dulaglutide 1.5 mg in body weight reduction. In 2023, these 10 drugs accounted for $56.2 billion in total Part D gross covered prescription drug costs—approximately 20% of all Part D gross drug spending—and were used by 8.8 million of the 54 million Medicare Part D enrollees [ev_000]. Beneficiary out‑of‑pocket spending on the selected drugs reached $3.9 billion in 2023, out of $18.9 billion in total Part D out‑of‑pocket costs [ev_000]. CMS estimated that had the negotiated MFPs been in place in 2023, Medicare net savings would have totaled $6 billion, representing a 22% reduction in net spending on those drugs [ev_000][ev_001]. When the negotiated prices take effect in January 2026, Medicare beneficiaries are projected to save an estimated $1.5 billion annually in out‑of‑pocket costs, over and above other IRA cost‑saving provisions such as the new out‑of‑pocket cap [ev_000][ev_001][ev_010]. The Congressional Budget Office projects that the drug negotiation provisions will reduce Medicare spending by $98.5 billion over the 10‑year period 2022–2031 [ev_010]. Pre‑negotiation spending data further illustrate the magnitude: in 2022, Part D spending on the same 10 drugs—which include widely used therapies such as Eliquis, Jardiance, Januvia, and Farxiga—was $46.4 billion, or 19% of all Part D expenditures [ev_013]. The negotiation program subsequently selected 15 additional Part D drugs for the second cycle, with prices effective in 2028; CMS preliminary estimates indicated “even greater” savings from these negotiations [ev_001]. Subsequent legislation (the Working Families Tax Cuts Act) further expanded orphan drug protections, potentially limiting the program’s reach by shielding certain products from negotiation [ev_011]; the Congressional Budget Office separately estimated that delaying negotiation for Keytruda and Opdivo due to orphan drug provisions would cost the federal government $8.8 billion over the coming decade [ev_001]. CMS issued final guidance for the second cycle in 2024 and released draft guidance for the third cycle in 2025, signaling the program’s continued expansion and refinement [ev_018][ev_019]. Overall, the initial negotiation cycle demonstrated substantial price reductions—reducing net Medicare spending by 22% and saving beneficiaries $1.5 billion—while subsequent cycles and legislative adjustments will shape long‑term savings trajectories [ev_000][ev_001][ev_010][ev_013][ev_018][ev_019].", "input_tokens": 12350, "output_tokens": 6080, "reasoning_tokens": 5413, "timestamp": "2026-05-23T08:40:58.078884+00:00"}

.codex\I-safety-002b\codex_gate_wiring_iter2.txt:17690:outputs\benchmark_p2_024\clinical\clinical_tirzepatide_t2dm\reasoning_trace.jsonl:2:{"call_id": "9a66998e31244f339c337e5259e5d541", "section": "Safety", "call_type": "section", "model": "deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro", "status": "ok", "content_source": "direct", "parent_call_id": null, "regen_reason": null, "attempt_n": 1, "reasoning_text": "We need to write the \"Safety\" section of a research report on tirzepatide, focusing on safety and tolerability, adverse events, and discontinuation rates. We must use only the provided evidence blocks, cite them properly, and follow the rules.\n\nFirst, let's inventory the evidence blocks relevant to safety:\n\n- ev_005 and ev_021: both refer to the same study (PMID 38512447) on tirzepatide in T1D. It's a retrospective study in 62 OW/OB T1D patients, plus 37 controls. Safety: \"There were no reported hospitalizations from severe hypoglycemia or diabetic ketoacidosis.\" This is a small pilot study, not a primary phase 3 trial for T2D. But it's relevant for safety in T1D, which is off-label. The question is about safety profile of tirzepatide across clinical studies, so we can include this as a specific population.\n\n- ev_001: SURPASS-2 trial (tirzepatide vs semaglutide in T2D). This is a T1 primary RCT. It provides detailed safety data: adverse events, discontinuation rates, gastrointestinal events, serious adverse events, deaths. We can extract exact numbers.\n\n- ev_018: SURMOUNT-3 press release (T5). Provides safety data: most common AEs were GI-related, mild to moderate. Specific percentages: nausea 39.7% vs 14.0%, diarrhea 31.0% vs 9.2%, constipation 23.0% vs 6.8%, vomiting 18.1% vs 1.4%. Adverse events leading to discontinuation: 10.5% tirzepatide vs 2.1% placebo. In a small retrospective study of 62 overweight or obese adults with type 1 diabetes treated with tirzepatide off-label, no hospitalizations for severe hypoglycemia or diabetic ketoacidosis were reported over 1 year [ev_005][ev_021]. A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized clinical trials confirmed that tirzepatide was associated with gastrointestinal adverse events as the most common tolerability issue, with rates of nausea ranging from 12% to 40% across trials, vomiting 2% to 18%, and diarrhea 12% to 31%, depending on dose and population [ev_006]. (ev_000 states this directly).\n\nIndirect comparison: An adjusted indirect treatment comparison between tirzepatide 5-15 mg and semaglutide 2 mg (highest approved dose for T2DM) found that tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg significantly reduced weight by an additional 3.15-5.15 kg and HbA1c by 0.36-0.4% more than semaglutide 2 mg [ev_000].\n\nSafety: In SURPASS-2, rates of any adverse event, serious adverse events, and discontinuations due to adverse events were similar between groups, though tirzepatide 15 mg had slightly higher discontinuation (8.5% vs 4.1% for semaglutide) [ev_001]. Nausea occurred in 17.4-22.1% vs 17.9%, vomiting in 5.7-9.8% vs 8.3%, and diarrhea in 13.2-16.4% vs 11.5% [ev_001]. The overall gastrointestinal side-effect profile of tirzepatide is similar to that of GLP-1 receptor agonists [ev_000].\n\nOther comparators: In SURPASS-3, tirzepatide compared to insulin degludec, and in SURPASS-4, to insulin glargine, with greater HbA1c reductions (1.93-2.58% vs 1.34-1.44%) and weight loss (7.5-12.9 kg loss vs 2.3 gain to 1.9 kg gain) [ev_000][ev_006]. At 40 weeks, estimated mean HbA1c reductions were –2.01, –2.24, and –2.30 percentage points with tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg, respectively, versus –1.86 percentage points with semaglutide, yielding treatment differences of –0.15 (95% CI –0.28 to –0.03; P=0.02), –0.39 (95% CI –0.51 to –0.26; P<0.001), and –0.45 (95% CI –0.57 to –0.32; P<0.001) [ev_001][ev_000][ev_006]. Body weight decreased by 7.8 kg, 10.3 kg, and 12.4 kg with tirzepatide, compared with 6.2 kg for semaglutide [ev_001][ev_000][ev_006]. The proportions attaining HbA1c <7.0% were 82–86% for tirzepatide versus 79% for semaglutide; HbA1c ≤6.5% was achieved by 69–80% versus 64%, and HbA1c <5.7% by 27–46% versus 19% [ev_001]. A composite endpoint of HbA1c ≤6.5%, ≥10% weight loss, and no clinically significant hypoglycemia was met by 22% of semaglutide‑treated patients and by 32–60% across tirzepatide dose groups [ev_000][ev_002]. An adjusted indirect treatment comparison estimated that tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg reduced HbA1c by an additional 0.36–0.4 percentage points and weight by an additional 3.15–5.15 kg relative to semaglutide 2 mg, the highest approved dose for type 2 diabetes [ev_000]. Safety profiles were broadly similar, with gastrointestinal events being most common [ev_001][ev_000][ev_006]. In SURPASS-2, nausea occurred in 17.4%, 19.2%, and 22.1% of patients receiving tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg, respectively, versus 17.9% with semaglutide; diarrhea in 13.2%, 16.4%, 13.8% versus 11.5%; and vomiting in 5.7%, 8.5%, 9.8% versus 8.3% [ev_001]. Treatment discontinuation due to adverse events was numerically higher with tirzepatide (6.0%, 8.5%, 8.5% for 5, 10, 15 mg) compared with semaglutide (4.1%) [ev_001]. In SURPASS-3, an open‑label, 52‑week trial of 1444 adults with type 2 diabetes inadequately controlled on metformin (baseline HbA1c 8.17%, weight 94.3 kg), tirzepatide 5–15 mg lowered HbA1c by 1.93% to 2.37% versus 1.34% with insulin degludec, while weight decreased by 7.5 to 12.9 kg versus a 2.3 kg gain [ev_000][ev_006]. The SURPASS-4 trial enrolled 2002 patients with type 2 diabetes and increased cardiovascular risk (baseline HbA1c 8.52%, weight 90.3 kg); over 52 weeks, tirzepatide reduced HbA1c by 2.24%–2.58% compared with 1.44% for insulin glargine, with weight losses of 7.1–11.7 kg versus a 1.9 kg gain [ev_000][ev_006]. In the double‑blind, 52‑week SURPASS J‑mono trial among 636 Japanese adults (baseline HbA1c 8.2%, weight 78.2 kg), tirzepatide lowered HbA1c by 2.4%–2.8% compared to 1.3% with dulaglutide 0.75 mg [ev_000][ev_006]. Overall, tirzepatide demonstrated superior glycemic control and weight reduction compared with semaglutide 1 mg, basal insulins, and dulaglutide across the SURPASS program, with a gastrointestinal tolerability profile akin to that of GLP-1 receptor agonists [ev_000][ev_006]. As a dual GIP/GLP-1 receptor agonist, tirzepatide produced greater weight loss than single GLP-1 receptor agonists in clinical settings [ev_015].", "input_tokens": 29743, "output_tokens": 8722, "reasoning_tokens": 7571, "timestamp": "2026-05-23T07:51:04.137092+00:00"}

The focus: \"Examine the relationship between tirzepatide dose (5, 10, 15 mg) and outcomes of glycemic control and weight reduction.\"\n\nWe must use only evidence in the blocks. When we name a specific study like SURPASS-2, we must provide full frame: N, baseline, comparator, endpoint, timepoint, etc. ev_001 already gives that: N=1879, baseline HbA1c 8.28%, weight 93.7 kg, vs semaglutide 1 mg, 40-week. So we can frame it well.\n\nFor dose response, we can say: higher doses lead to greater reductions in HbA1c and weight, both in T2D and obesity trials. For T2D, SURPASS-2: 5 mg, 10 mg, 15 mg gave HbA1c changes of -2.01, -2.24, -2.30 percentage points; weight loss -7.8 kg, -10.3 kg, -12.4 kg, all vs semaglutide (-1.86% and -6.2 kg) [ev_001]. The meta-analysis ev_006 shows dose-dependent HbA1c and weight reductions across SURPASS trials: e.g., SURPASS-1 HbA1c -1.87, -1.89, -2.07 (5,10,15 mg), weight -7.0, -7.8, -9.5 kg; SURPASS-3: -1.93, -2.20, -2.37% HbA1c; weight -7.5, -10.7, -12.9 kg; SURPASS-4: -2.24, -2.43, -2.58% HbA1c; weight -7.1, -9.5, -11.7 kg; SURPASS-5: -2.11, -2.40, -2.34% HbA1c (maybe not monotonic), weight -5.4, -7.5, -8.8 kg; SURPASS-6: -1.92, -2.15, -2.27% HbA1c, weight -6.7, -9.2, -11.0 kg; etc. So dose-dependent.\n\nFor obesity without diabetes, SURMOUNT-1: weight change -15.0%, -19.5%, -20.9% at 5,10,15 mg (from ev_002/", "content_text": "In the SURPASS-2 phase 3, open-label trial, which randomized 1879 participants with baseline HbA1c 8.28% and mean weight 93.7 kg to receive tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, 15 mg, or semaglutide 1 mg, the primary endpoint—change in HbA1c from baseline to 40 weeks—showed a clear dose-dependent glycemic reduction across the tirzepatide arms [ev_001]. Specifically, tirzepatide 5 mg reduced HbA1c by −2.01 percentage points, tirzepatide 10 mg by −2.24 percentage points, and tirzepatide 15 mg by −2.30 percentage points, compared to −1.86 percentage points with semaglutide [ev_001]. The corresponding treatment differences versus semaglutide were −0.15 percentage points (95% CI −0.28 to −0.03, P=0.02) for 5 mg, −0.39 percentage points (95% CI −0.51 to −0.26, P<0.001) for 10 mg, and −0.45 percentage points (95% CI −0.57 to −0.32, P<0.001) for 15 mg, confirming escalating efficacy with higher doses [ev_001]. This dose-response pattern is corroborated across the SURPASS program: one narrative review reports that once-weekly tirzepatide 5–15 mg reduced HbA1c by 1.87% to 3.02% across populations with T2DM, encompassing monotherapy, dual therapy, and insulin add-on settings [ev_000][ev_017]. A meta-analysis of SURPASS trials further quantifies the dose-ordering, showing that in SURPASS-1 (N=705, 40 weeks, versus placebo) HbA1c changes were −1.87% (5 mg), −1.89% (10 mg), and −2.07% (15 mg); in SURPASS-3 (N=1437, 52 weeks, versus insulin degludec) changes were −1.93%, −2.20%, and −2.37%; and in SURPASS-4 (N=1995, 52 weeks, versus insulin glargine) changes were −2.24%, −2.43%, and −2.58%, respectively, all demonstrating progressively greater glycemic improvement with increasing dose [ev_006].\n\nFor weight reduction, the dose-response relationship is equally pronounced. In SURPASS-2, body weight decreased by 7.8 kg with tirzepatide 5 mg, 10.3 kg with 10 mg, and 12.4 kg with 15 mg, versus 6.2 kg with semaglutide 1 mg [ev_001][ev_006]. A meta-analysis of the SURPASS program documents consistent dose-ordered weight loss across trials: in SURPASS-1, weight reductions were −7.0 kg (5 mg), −7.8 kg (10 mg), and −9.5 kg (15 mg); in SURPASS-3, −7.5 kg, −10.7 kg, and −12.9 kg; and in SURPASS-4, −7.1 kg, −9.5 kg, and −11.7 kg, respectively [ev_006]. In populations without diabetes, the SURMOUNT-1 trial (N=2539, 72 weeks, mean baseline weight 104.8 kg) demonstrated mean percentage weight changes of −15.0% with tirzepatide 5 mg, −19.5% with 10 mg, and −20.9% with 15 mg, compared to −3.1% with placebo [ev_002][ev_019]. The SURMOUNT-2 trial (N=938, 72 weeks, baseline HbA1c 8.03%, weight 100.7 kg) evaluated only the 10 mg and 15 mg doses in patients with T2DM and reported weight reductions of −12.8% and −14.7%, respectively, versus −3.2% with placebo [ev_003][ev_020]. A narrative review confirms that across the SURPASS and SURMOUNT programs, weight loss is dose-dependent, and the highest approved maintenance doses (10 mg and 15 mg) consistently produce greater weight reduction than the 5 mg dose [ev_000][ev_017]. In a real-world retrospective study of overweight or obese adults with type 1 diabetes (N=62), the mean weekly tirzepatide dose increased from 5.6 ± 1.9 mg at 3 months to 9.7 ± 3.3 mg at 1 year, and this dose escalation was associated with progressive reductions in BMI, weight, and HbA1c (sustained −0.67% at 1 year), along with significant improvements in continuous glucose monitoring metrics [ev_005][ev_021]. These findings collectively establish that tirzepatide's effects on both glycemic control and weight reduction are dose-responsive, with the 10 mg and 15 mg doses yielding the greatest magnitude of benefit across diverse populations and trial designs [ev_001][ev_006][ev_002][ev_019][ev_000][ev_017].", "input_tokens": 33598, "output_tokens": 2108, "reasoning_tokens": 961, "timestamp": "2026-05-23T07:54:10.112772+00:00"}

In SURPASS‑2, an open‑label 40‑week phase‑3 trial, N=1879 participants with type 2 diabetes (mean baseline HbA1c 8.28%, body weight 93.7 kg) were randomized to tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, 15 mg or semaglutide 1 mg; the primary endpoint of HbA1c change at 40 weeks showed reductions of −2.01, −2.24, and −2.30 percentage points with tirzepatide versus −1.86 with semaglutide [ev_001]. Estimated treatment differences versus semaglutide were −0.15, −0.39, and −0.45 percentage points, respectively (P<0.001 for the 10 mg and 15 mg doses) [ev_001]; corresponding body weight reductions were −7.8 kg, −10.3 kg, and −12.4 kg with tirzepatide compared with −6.2 kg with semaglutide [ev_006]. In SURPASS‑1, a double‑blind, placebo‑controlled 40‑week trial, N=705 drug‑naïve participants (baseline HbA1c 7.9%, weight 85.9 kg) received tirzepatide 5, 10, or 15 mg or placebo, with HbA1c declines of 1.87%, 1.89%, and 2.07% versus a 0.04% increase on placebo [ev_006][ev_000]. Across SURPASS‑3 (N=1444, baseline HbA1c 8.17%, weight 94.3 kg, comparator insulin degludec) and SURPASS‑4 (N=1995, baseline HbA1c 8.52%, weight 90.3 kg, comparator insulin glargine), tirzepatide 5–15 mg reduced HbA1c by 1.93–2.37% and 2.24–2.58%, respectively, significantly exceeding the basal insulin arms [ev_006][ev_000]. Weight loss in these head‑to‑head trials ranged from −7.5 to −12.9 kg (SURPASS‑3) and −7.1 to −11.7 kg (SURPASS‑4), in contrast to weight gain or minimal reduction with insulin comparators [ev_006]. When added to insulin glargine in SURPASS‑5 (N=475, baseline HbA1c 8.31%, weight 95.2 kg), tirzepatide lowered HbA1c by 2.11–2.40% versus 0.86% with placebo at 40 weeks, with concomitant weight losses of 5.4–8.8 kg [ev_006][ev_000]. In Japanese populations (SURPASS J‑mono), HbA1c reductions reached 2.4–3.02% with tirzepatide, exceeding those seen with dulaglutide 0.75 mg [ev_000]. The proportion of participants achieving HbA1c <7.0% exceeded 80% with tirzepatide across the program; in SURPASS‑2, 82–86% of tirzepatide‑treated patients reached this target compared with 79% on semaglutide [ev_001][ev_006]. In the SURMOUNT‑2 trial, which enrolled 938 adults with type 2 diabetes and obesity (baseline HbA1c 8.03%, weight 100.7 kg), tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg produced placebo‑adjusted HbA1c reductions of approximately −2.1% at 72 weeks and body‑weight reductions of −12.8% and −14.7% versus −3.2% with placebo [ev_003][ev_002]. A composite endpoint of HbA1c ≤6.5% plus ≥10% weight loss without clinically significant hypoglycemia was achieved by 32–60% of tirzepatide‑treated patients in SURPASS‑2, compared with 22% in the semaglutide group [ev_000]. One review reports an indirect treatment comparison suggesting that tirzepatide 10 and 15 mg reduce HbA1c by an additional 0.36–0.40% and weight by 3.15–5.15 kg relative to semaglutide 2 mg [ev_000]. A meta‑analysis of randomized trials confirmed that tirzepatide 15 mg consistently provided the greatest HbA1c reductions (weighted mean difference approximately −2.0% to −2.5% versus placebo or active comparators) and dose‑dependent increments in weight loss [ev_006].", "input_tokens": 34602, "output_tokens": 6254, "reasoning_tokens": 5146, "timestamp": "2026-05-23T07:54:34.102256+00:00"}

In the SURPASS program, treatment periods extended up to 104 weeks, during which once-weekly tirzepatide 5 to 15 mg reduced HbA1c by 1.87% to 3.02% and body weight by 5.4 to 12.9 kg across the T2DM spectrum [ev_017]. \n\nFor weight durability, a pre-planned pooled analysis of two phase-3 trials reported body-weight outcomes for longer durations: one source in the evidence specifies that in SURPASS-4, weight loss and improvement in HbA1c was maintained for 2 years with tirzepatide, providing sustained benefits [ev_000]. In a real-world retrospective study of 62 overweight or obese adults with type 1 diabetes receiving tirzepatide, HbA1c decreased as early as 3 months and was sustained through 1-year follow-up (-0.67% at 1 year), with weight loss of 18.5% (>46 pounds) at 1 year [ev_005][ev_021]. \n\nIn the SURMOUNT-3 trial, a double-blind, placebo-controlled study that randomized 579 adults with obesity or overweight (without diabetes) who achieved ≥5.0% weight reduction after a 12-week intensive lifestyle intervention to tirzepatide maximum tolerated dose (10 or 15 mg) or placebo once weekly for 72 weeks, the additional mean percent weight change from randomization to week 72 was -18.4% with tirzepatide versus 2.5% with placebo (estimated treatment difference -20.8 percentage points, 95% CI -23.2% to -18.5%, P < 0.001) [ev_016]. A secondary endpoint from SURMOUNT-3 showed a total mean weight loss of 26.6% (29.2 kg) from study entry over 84 weeks with tirzepatide [ev_018]. \n\nAmong participants in the SURMOUNT-4 trial, a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study in 670 adults with obesity or overweight without diabetes, the systematic review reports that participants were on maximum tolerated dose of tirzepatide versus placebo for 88 weeks, with mean baseline BMI 38.4 kg/m² and HbA1c 5.5% [ev_006]. The SURMOUNT-4 trial findings underscore that continued treatment with tirzepatide is critical for weight maintenance, as one source highlights that ongoing therapy sustains weight reduction over extended treatment periods [ev_014]. Additional evidence from the SURMOUNT-1 trial, which evaluated tirzepatide 5, 10, and 15 mg versus placebo over 72 weeks in 2539 obese participants without diabetes, demonstrated mean percentage changes in weight at week 72 of -15.0%, -19.5%, and -20.9%, respectively, and -3.1% with placebo (P < 0.001) [ev_019][ev_000]. Across the SURPASS and SURMOUNT programs, tirzepatide showed superior maintenance of glycemic control and weight reduction compared to placebo, basal insulins, dulaglutide, and semaglutide 1 mg over treatment durations of 40 to 104 weeks [ev_017][ev_000][ev_006].", "input_tokens": 30531, "output_tokens": 1764, "reasoning_tokens": 961, "timestamp": "2026-05-23T07:56:40.768271+00:00"}

Adverse events leading to treatment discontinuation were 6.0% (atom_016), 8.5% (atom_017), 8.5% (atom_018), and 4.1% (atom_019) across the same four arms [ev_000]. I'll include multiple citations for same claim where supported.", "content_text": "Tirzepatide, a dual glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide (GIP) and glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) receptor agonist, has demonstrated robust improvements in both glycemic control and body weight in adults with type 2 diabetes across its pivotal phase‑3 program [ev_000][ev_001][ev_006]. A systematic review and meta-analysis of six randomized controlled trials (N=6,579) reported that tirzepatide reduced glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) by a weighted mean difference of −1.07% (95% CI −1.44 to −0.56) (atom_160) [ev_001], fasting serum glucose by −21.50 mg/dL (95% CI −34.44 to −8.56) (atom_162) [ev_001], and body weight by −7.99 kg (95% CI −11.36 to −4.62) (atom_163) [ev_001]. Weight loss in the tirzepatide arms ranged from 7.25 kg to 10.36 kg (atom_169, atom_170) [ev_001]; monotherapy produced a 7.40 kg reduction while add-on therapy yielded 8.11 kg (atom_171, atom_172) [ev_001]. The proportion of patients achieving clinically meaningful weight‑loss thresholds was consistently greater with tirzepatide, including attainment of ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15% body‑weight reduction (atom_173, atom_174, atom_175) [ev_001].\n\nIn the SURPASS‑2 trial (N=1,879, randomized to tirzepatide 5 mg, 10 mg, or 15 mg once weekly versus semaglutide 1 mg), baseline mean HbA1c was 8.28% and mean body weight was 93.7 kg (atom_104, atom_105) [ev_000]. The primary endpoint of HbA1c change at 40 weeks showed clear dose‑dependent superiority of tirzepatide over semaglutide [ev_000], and a greater proportion of patients treated with tirzepatide attained the stringent HbA1c targets of <7.0%, ≤6.5%, and <5.7% compared with those receiving semaglutide, with the improvements increasing across the 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg doses [ev_000]. Similarly, a higher proportion achieved weight‑loss thresholds of ≥5%, ≥10%, and ≥15% in the tirzepatide arms versus semaglutide [ev_006]. The trial also assessed a composite efficacy endpoint of HbA1c ≤6.5% plus ≥10% weight loss without clinically significant hypoglycemia, highlighting the combined metabolic benefit attainable with tirzepatide (atom_122, atom_123) [ev_000].\n\nIn the SURMOUNT‑2 trial (N=938, enrolling adults with type 2 diabetes and obesity, baseline HbA1c 7–10%) [ev_002], tirzepatide 10 mg and 15 mg produced ≥5% weight loss in 79–83% of participants, compared with 32% for placebo (atom_200, atom_201) [ev_002][ev_021]; clinically meaningful HbA1c reductions were also observed [ev_002][ev_021]. Across the broader SURPASS program, tirzepatide consistently demonstrated HbA1c reductions and weight loss versus active comparators including insulin degludec, insulin glargine, and dulaglutide, with a favorable glycemic‑control profile both as monotherapy and as add‑on to metformin or insulin [ev_006][ev_017][ev_001].", "input_tokens": 38796, "output_tokens": 6888, "reasoning_tokens": 6092, "timestamp": "2026-05-27T04:27:36.952893+00:00"}

The text establishes that untreated PPS has a surface energy of approximately 38 dynes/cm, requiring plasma pretreatment to reach 60-70 dynes/cm for robust bonding, exceeding the minimum 48-54 dynes/cm calculated for epoxy wetting. Methodologies include Electrical Corona Discharge, Atmospheric Blown Ion, Flame Plasma, and RF Cold Gas, with RF Cold Gas noted for hydrocarbon removal without pre-cleaning. This overlaps with the research question's focus on plasma-activated substrates, though the substrate here is PPS rather than polypropylene or aluminum oxide. Adhesive curing occurs at 300-350 degrees Fahrenheit, ensuring full crosslinking for heat-deflection temperatures above 500 degrees Fahrenheit. The article emphasizes that insufficient cure is a common failure mode, a variable critical when comparing experimental strengths to theoretical predictions. Glass-filled variants (30-40 percent) are discussed, introducing surface texture variables affecting mechanical interlocking. While the adhesive system is epoxy rather than PEI/DVS, the surface energy targets and plasma necessity provide a comparative baseline for activation intensity. Confidence in the plasma methodology is MEDIUM, as these are established industry practices, but confidence in relevance to the specific research question is LOW due to material mismatch. It adds understanding regarding the criticality of mold temperature (275-300 degrees Fahrenheit) on crystallization, which affects surface appearance and bonding. No molecular dynamics predictions are offered, limiting direct comparison to the research question's theoretical component. The industry perspective dominates, focusing on practical manufacturing constraints rather than fundamental bond physics.\n\n    (Word count check: ~250 words). The study reports adhesion strengths of 3.7 MPa on stainless steel, 3.1 MPa on glass, and 2.7 MPa on copper, while noting weaker forces on low surface energy substrates like polypropylene (PP) and PTFE. This directly addresses the substrate variable in the research question, confirming PP as a challenging substrate for adhesion without specific activation described here. The work combines experimental results with theoretical calculations, identifying strong interaction energy between POMs and polymer as the main factor for performance, paralleling the research question's interest in comparing measured strengths with molecular dynamics predictions. The adhesive tolerates temperatures from -196 to 55 degrees Celsius, exceeding commercial hot melt adhesives. Methodology includes FT-IR, PXRD, XPS, and NMR to confirm structure, with crosslink density measured at 1.55 × 10^-4 mol mL^-1 for the adhesive versus 69.93 × 10^-4 mol mL^-1 for PEG. Confidence in the quantitative adhesion data is HIGH, but relevance to DVS crosslinked PEI is LOW due to chemical differences. The study highlights that adhesion failure occurs mainly at the interface, indicating high cohesion interaction. This contrasts with the research question's focus on crosslinked PEI coatings, yet the methodological approach of validating theoretical interaction energy against macroscopic shear strength is highly relevant. The article adds understanding regarding the impact of crosslink density on adhesion performance and the viability of solvent-free processing. It does not mention divinyl sulfone or polyethylenimine, representing a significant chemical divergence. However, the demonstration that theoretical calculations can explain low-temperature tolerance offers a framework for how MD predictions might correlate with experimental lap-shear data in other systems. The emerging trend of solvent-free processing is noted as advantageous for scale-up.\n\n    (Word count check: ~280 words).    1: """I-meta-006 (#1006) — system-agnostic ATOMIC claim + citation extraction.
   2: 
   3: Turns ANY deep-research report (POLARIS, ChatGPT, Gemini) into a uniform list of
   4: ATOMIC factual claims, each carrying the citation(s) it attaches to, so the SAME
   5: §-1.1 faithfulness audit runs identically across systems (Codex design-gate
   6: APPROVE iter3). It assigns NO verdicts and confers NO auto-pass — POLARIS
   7: `[#ev:]` spans are used for extraction + to DEFINE the cited span, never to
   8: auto-verify.
   9: 
  10: Cash-free + deterministic given the INJECTED ``atomizer`` (the real atomizer runs
  11: in the operator-gated paid run; tests pass a deterministic fake). Compound
  12: sentences split into separate factual atoms so the denominator is not
  13: methodology-dependent (Codex iter-1 P1-2). An atom with NO citation is KEPT (it
  14: scores as uncited downstream — Codex iter-1 P2-1).
  15: 
  16: Citation formats handled:
  17:   - POLARIS: ``[#ev:<id>:<start>-<end>]`` (the exact span) + bare ``[N]`` →
  18:     bibliography; reuses ``provenance_generator.parse_provenance_tokens``.
  19:   - ChatGPT / Gemini: academic ``(Author, Year)`` keys + numbered superscripts,
  20:     resolved against the report's reference list.
  21: """
  22: from __future__ import annotations
  23: 
  24: import re
  25: from dataclasses import dataclass, field
  26: from typing import Callable
  27: 
  28: from src.polaris_graph.generator.provenance_generator import (
  29:     parse_provenance_tokens,
  30:     split_into_sentences,
  31: )
  32: 
  33: # A system identifier.
  34: SYSTEMS = ("polaris", "chatgpt", "gemini")
  35: 
  36: # academic "(Author, 2015)" / "(Acemoglu & Restrepo, 2018, 2019)" inline keys.
  37: _AUTHOR_YEAR_RE = re.compile(r"\(([^()]*?\b(?:19|20)\d{2}[a-z]?[^()]*?)\)")
  38: # bare numbered citation markers: [1], [12].
  39: _NUMBERED_RE = re.compile(r"\[(\d{1,3})\]")
  40: # unicode superscript citation markers (Gemini-style), e.g. "churn²⁷".
  41: _SUPERSCRIPT_RE = re.compile(r"[⁰¹²³⁴-⁹]+")
  42: _SUPERSCRIPT_MAP = {
  43:     "⁰": "0", "¹": "1", "²": "2", "³": "3", "⁴": "4",
  44:     "⁵": "5", "⁶": "6", "⁷": "7", "⁸": "8", "⁹": "9",
  45: }
  46: # strip POLARIS provenance + bare numbered markers from the rendered atom text.
  47: _POLARIS_TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"\[#ev:[A-Za-z0-9_]+:\d+-\d+\]")
  48: _ATOM_HAS_ALPHA_RE = re.compile(r"[A-Za-z]")
  49: 
  50: 
  51: def _superscript_to_number(run: str) -> str:
  52:     return "".join(_SUPERSCRIPT_MAP.get(c, "") for c in run)
  53: 
  54: 
  55: @dataclass(frozen=True)
  56: class CitationRef:
  57:     """One citation a claim attaches to. ``kind='uncited'`` = no citation."""
  58:     system: str
  59:     kind: str                 # "ev_span" | "author_year" | "numbered" | "uncited"
  60:     raw_key: str              # the marker text as it appeared in the report
  61:     resolved: str | None = None   # url / evidence_id / reference text (None = unresolved)
  62:     ev_start: int | None = None   # POLARIS [#ev:] span (the MOST SPECIFIC cited anchor)
  63:     ev_end: int | None = None
  64: 
  65: 
  66: @dataclass
  67: class ExtractedAtom:
  68:     atom_id: str
  69:     text: str                       # clean prose, citation markers stripped
  70:     citation_refs: list[CitationRef] = field(default_factory=list)
  71: 
  72:     @property
  73:     def is_cited(self) -> bool:
  74:         return any(c.kind != "uncited" for c in self.citation_refs)
  75: 
  76: 
  77: def _default_atomizer(sentence: str) -> list[str]:
  78:     """Conservative deterministic fallback: split independent clauses on a
  79:     semicolon. The real run injects a faithful atomizer; this keeps the harness
  80:     deterministic + cash-free for the obvious compound case without inventing
  81:     atoms."""
  82:     parts = [p.strip() for p in sentence.split(";")]
  83:     return [p for p in parts if p]
  84: 
  85: 
  86: def _strip_markers(text: str, system: str) -> str:
  87:     s = _POLARIS_TOKEN_RE.sub("", text)
  88:     s = _NUMBERED_RE.sub("", s)
  89:     if system != "polaris":
  90:         s = _AUTHOR_YEAR_RE.sub("", s)
  91:         s = _SUPERSCRIPT_RE.sub("", s)
  92:     return re.sub(r"\s+", " ", s).strip()
  93: 
  94: 
  95: def _extract_citations(
  96:     sentence: str, system: str, references: dict[str, str],
  97: ) -> list[CitationRef]:
  98:     refs: list[CitationRef] = []
  99:     if system == "polaris":
 100:         for tok in parse_provenance_tokens(sentence):
 101:             refs.append(CitationRef(
 102:                 system=system, kind="ev_span", raw_key=tok.raw,
 103:                 resolved=tok.evidence_id, ev_start=tok.start, ev_end=tok.end,
 104:             ))
 105:         for m in _NUMBERED_RE.finditer(sentence):
 106:             num = m.group(1)
 107:             refs.append(CitationRef(
 108:                 system=system, kind="numbered", raw_key=m.group(0),
 109:                 resolved=references.get(num),
 110:             ))
 111:     else:
 112:         for m in _AUTHOR_YEAR_RE.finditer(sentence):
 113:             key = m.group(1).strip()
 114:             refs.append(CitationRef(
 115:                 system=system, kind="author_year", raw_key=m.group(0),
 116:                 resolved=references.get(key) or references.get(_norm_key(key)),
 117:             ))
 118:         for m in _NUMBERED_RE.finditer(sentence):
 119:             num = m.group(1)
 120:             refs.append(CitationRef(
 121:                 system=system, kind="numbered", raw_key=m.group(0),
 122:                 resolved=references.get(num),
 123:             ))
 124:         for m in _SUPERSCRIPT_RE.finditer(sentence):
 125:             num = _superscript_to_number(m.group(0))
 126:             if num:
 127:                 refs.append(CitationRef(
 128:                     system=system, kind="numbered", raw_key=m.group(0),
 129:                     resolved=references.get(num),
 130:                 ))
 131:     if not refs:
 132:         refs.append(CitationRef(system=system, kind="uncited", raw_key=""))
 133:     return refs
 134: 
 135: 
 136: def _norm_key(key: str) -> str:
 137:     return re.sub(r"\s+", " ", key.strip().lower())
 138: 
 139: 
 140: def extract_atoms(
 141:     report_text: str,
 142:     system: str,
 143:     references: dict[str, str] | None = None,
 144:     *,
 145:     atomizer: Callable[[str], list[str]] | None = None,
 146:     question_id: str = "",
 147: ) -> list[ExtractedAtom]:
 148:     """Extract atomic claims + their citations from a report. ``references`` maps
 149:     a citation key (numbered string / author-year / normalized author-year) to a
 150:     resolved source (url/title); ``atomizer`` is the INJECTED faithful atomizer
 151:     (default = conservative semicolon split)."""
 152:     if system not in SYSTEMS:
 153:         raise ValueError(f"unknown system {system!r}; expected one of {SYSTEMS}")
 154:     references = references or {}
 155:     atomize = atomizer or _default_atomizer
 156: 
 157:     atoms: list[ExtractedAtom] = []
 158:     for si, sentence in enumerate(split_into_sentences(report_text)):
 159:         refs = _extract_citations(sentence, system, references)
 160:         for ai, raw_atom in enumerate(atomize(sentence)):
 161:             text = _strip_markers(raw_atom, system)
 162:             if not text or not _ATOM_HAS_ALPHA_RE.search(text):
 163:                 continue
 164:             prefix = f"{system}:{question_id}:" if question_id else f"{system}:"
 165:             atoms.append(ExtractedAtom(
 166:                 atom_id=f"{prefix}{si}:{ai}", text=text, citation_refs=list(refs),
 167:             ))
 168:     return atoms

 succeeded in 9667ms:
 958: def apply_full_capability_benchmark_slate() -> None:
 959:     """Make the full-capability slate AUTHORITATIVE over .env / conservative defaults (FLOOR semantics).
 960: 
 961:     Codex diff-gate iter-2 P1-1: ``setdefault`` was WRONG. ``load_dotenv`` (openrouter_client import)
 962:     puts .env values into ``os.environ`` BEFORE this slate runs, so a conservative .env default
 963:     (e.g. ``PG_MOST_MAX_EVIDENCE=300``, ``PG_AGENTIC_WEB_PER_ROUND=6``) SURVIVED the setdefault and
 964:     silently throttled the benchmark below the slate. Fix: every numeric key is a **FLOOR** —
 965:     ``max(existing, slate)`` — so a HIGHER operator/.env value (more capability, e.g.
 966:     ``PG_LLM_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=600`` in .env) is KEPT, but a LOWER default is RAISED to full capability.
 967:     No silent downgrade in either direction (operator no-downgrade directive). Feature flags in
 968:     ``_BENCHMARK_FORCE_ON_FLAGS`` are FORCED ON (a benchmark feature off is a capability downgrade).
 969:     Exact-value flags in ``_BENCHMARK_FORCE_EXACT_FLAGS`` are forced to their slate value.
 970: 
 971:     Codex diff-gate iter-2 P1-2: MUST run BEFORE the sweep / live_retriever / state imports, which cache
 972:     ``PG_LIVE_CONTENT_MAX`` / ``PG_LIVE_HTTP_TIMEOUT`` / ``PG_AGENTIC_WEB_PER_ROUND`` as import-time module
 973:     constants. ``main()`` applies it before ``load_locked_questions()``; ``run_gate_b_query`` applies it
 974:     before the sweep import. ``preflight_full_capability`` then validates the LIVE module constants so a
 975:     too-late application is caught fail-closed.
 976:     """
 977:     for name, value in _FULL_CAPABILITY_BENCHMARK_SLATE.items():
 978:         if name in _BENCHMARK_FORCE_ON_FLAGS or name in _BENCHMARK_FORCE_EXACT_FLAGS:
 979:             os.environ[name] = value                       # force exact value — no silent benchmark drift
 980:             continue
 981:         try:
 982:             current = float(os.environ.get(name, value))
 983:         except (TypeError, ValueError):
 984:             current = float(value)
 985:         os.environ[name] = str(int(max(current, float(value))))   # FLOOR: raise-to-slate, keep-if-higher
 986:     # PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN is an import-time constant in openrouter_client (cached before this slate via
 987:     # the role import chain), so the floor above only fixes the env; sync the live module global too so
 988:     # check_run_budget enforces the floored cap and run_one_query's manifest reads it (Codex iter-1 P1-2).
 989:     from src.polaris_graph.llm.openrouter_client import set_max_cost_per_run
 990:     set_max_cost_per_run(float(os.environ["PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN"]))
